data.title: Academy Sports Ongoing Sales And Cash Flow Strains Test Growth Story
data.description:
• Academy Sports and Outdoors (NasdaqGS:ASO) is reporting ongoing annual sales declines alongside weak same store performance.
  • The company is also seeing a reduced free cash flow margin, pointing to more capital intensive operations.
  • These pressures raise questions around product mix, customer engagement and the effectiveness of current management decisions.

Academy Sports and Outdoors, a large sporting goods and outdoor retailer, operates in a segment where shoppers can increasingly compare prices and assortment across big box chains and online platforms. For investors looking at NasdaqGS:ASO, the combination of softer sales trends and a thinner free cash flow margin suggests the core business is under pressure at the store level and in the cash generation profile. That mix can matter if the company wants flexibility for store refreshes, merchandising changes or e commerce investments.

Looking ahead, you may want to watch how the company talks about merchandise categories, store traffic and capital spending priorities in upcoming updates. Any shifts in promotion intensity, store format or inventory approach could be important signals for how NasdaqGS:ASO is responding to competitive pressures and potential changes in customer behavior.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Academy Sports and Outdoors by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Academy Sports and Outdoors.

See which insiders are buying and buying and selling Academy Sports and Outdoors following this latest news.

For existing and prospective shareholders, the 2.4% annual sales decline over the last three years and a 3.9 percentage point drop in free cash flow margin are clear signals that the core retail model is under pressure. Weak same-store performance suggests existing locations are not pulling their weight, which can limit the benefits of new-store openings and e-commerce growth. At the same time, a lower free cash flow margin indicates more cash is being tied up in inventory, store build-outs or other capital-heavy projects, just as competition from retailers like Dick’s Sporting Goods and big-box chains such as Walmart and Target keeps pricing tight. With Academy Sports and Outdoors preparing to report its fourth-quarter and fiscal 2025 results on 17 March 2026, this combination of softer sales and a more capital intensive profile is likely to be a key focus for investors assessing how resilient the current strategy really is.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Academy Sports and Outdoors Narrative

  • The recent pressure on sales and free cash flow directly tests the narrative that store expansion and e-commerce growth can support a stronger, more scalable retail model.
  • Weak same-store sales and a thinner free cash flow margin challenge expectations that private-label development, loyalty programs and supply-chain investments will support stable margins and customer growth.
  • The apparent increase in capital intensity and competition-driven promotions may not be fully reflected in the existing narrative’s assumptions around operating leverage and cost savings.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Academy Sports and Outdoors to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Ongoing same-store sales weakness raises the risk that new-store openings could dilute overall productivity if customer demand does not keep pace.
  • ⚠️ A 3.9 percentage point reduction in free cash flow margin suggests less financial flexibility for buybacks, dividends or reinvestment if capital needs stay elevated.
  • 🎁 Analysts highlight that Academy Sports and Outdoors is trading at what they view as good value compared to peers and the wider specialty retail industry.
  • 🎁 Forecast earnings growth of 6.39% per year, if achieved, could help offset current operational pressures and support future reinvestment.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, it is worth watching whether upcoming results show any stabilization in same-store sales and whether management outlines a plan to improve free cash flow, for example through tighter inventory management or moderated capital spending. Commentary around promotional activity, customer mix and competitive dynamics versus retailers like Dick’s Sporting Goods and Walmart will also be important for judging how sustainable Academy Sports and Outdoors’ current playbook is. The tone and detail on future store openings, e-commerce investments and potential cost savings will help you gauge whether today’s pressure is temporary or part of a longer trend.

data.title: Is Carrier Global (CARR) Fairly Priced After Recent 34% Three Year Share Price Gain
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether Carrier Global's current share price lines up with its underlying worth, this valuation focused breakdown is designed to give you a clear and structured view.
  • The stock recently closed at US$61.17, with returns of 2.7% over the last 30 days, 14.3% year to date and 34.2% over 3 years, which may have some investors reassessing both its growth potential and risk profile.
  • Recent coverage has focused on Carrier Global's position as a major player in heating, ventilation and air conditioning, and how its scale and brand recognition keep it central to discussions about building efficiency and climate control solutions. These themes often frame how investors interpret the share price moves you have seen over the past weeks and years.
  • On our checks based valuation framework, Carrier Global currently scores 2 out of 6. Next we will look at how different valuation approaches view the stock and then finish by looking at a broader way to think about what valuation really tells you as an investor.

Carrier Global scores just 2/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Carrier Global Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a business could be worth by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those cash flows back to today to get a single present value figure.

For Carrier Global, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, based on cash flow projections in US$. The latest twelve month free cash flow stands at about US$2.0b. Analyst and extrapolated estimates suggest free cash flow reaching around US$3.1b by 2029, with a series of annual projections in between that are discounted back using a required rate of return.

Combining these discounted cash flows, the model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of US$66.10 per share. Compared with the recent share price of US$61.17, this implies a discount of about 7.5%, which the model interprets as a modest gap between price and estimated value rather than a dramatic mismatch.

Result: ABOUT RIGHT

Carrier Global is fairly valued according to our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF), but this can change at a moment's notice. Track the value in your watchlist or portfolio and be alerted on when to act.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Carrier Global.

Approach 2: Carrier Global Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies like Carrier Global, the P/E ratio is a useful way to check how much you are paying for each dollar of earnings. It links the share price directly to the business’s current earnings power, which most investors watch closely.

What counts as a “normal” P/E depends a lot on how the market views a company’s growth prospects and risk. Higher expected earnings growth or a business seen as more resilient can justify a higher P/E, while lower growth expectations or higher perceived risk usually point to a lower P/E.

Carrier Global is trading on a P/E of 35.14x, compared with an industry average of 23.00x for Building companies and a peer average of 30.82x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for Carrier Global is 40.85x. The Fair Ratio is a proprietary estimate of what a reasonable P/E could be for this company after taking into account factors such as its earnings growth profile, industry, profit margins, market cap and specific risks. This makes it a more tailored reference point than a simple comparison with peers or the broad industry, which may differ on growth, quality and risk.

On this basis, Carrier Global’s current P/E of 35.14x sits below the Fair Ratio of 40.85x, which indicates that the shares are trading at a discount to that company specific benchmark.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Upgrade Your Decision Making: Choose your Carrier Global Narrative

Earlier we mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation, so let us introduce you to Narratives, which are simply your own story about Carrier Global, tied to specific assumptions for future revenue, earnings, margins and a fair value that you can compare with today’s share price. On Simply Wall St’s Community page, millions of investors use Narratives to connect their view of the business to a forecast and then to a fair value, so they can see at a glance whether their story says the stock is above or below their number and decide how that lines up with their own timing. These Narratives update automatically as new data, news or earnings are added, so your fair value view stays current without you needing to rebuild a model each time. For Carrier Global, one user might lean closer to a higher fair value such as about US$89.13 if they think data center cooling, European heat pump adoption and aftermarket growth support that story, while another might anchor on a lower fair value such as about US$55.00 if they focus more on residential softness, factory under absorption and tighter P/E assumptions.

For Carrier Global, here are previews of two leading Carrier Global Narratives to make comparison easier:

🐂 Carrier Global Bull Case

Fair value in this narrative: US$71.66 per share

Implied discount to this fair value: about 14.6% compared to the recent price of US$61.17

Revenue growth assumption in this narrative: 4.12% per year

  • Focuses on Carrier Global capturing demand in sustainable energy, heat pumps and data center cooling, with products such as advanced commercial heat pumps and AI enabled home energy management.
  • Builds in higher margins over time, supported by aftermarket services, Viessmann integration and cost efficiency efforts.
  • Anchors on analyst assumptions for revenue, earnings and P/E that together support a fair value above the recent share price, while pointing out regional and tariff risks that investors may want to monitor.
🐻 Carrier Global Bear Case

  • Assumes a lower future P/E multiple of 18.2x, paired with more modest revenue growth, even though earnings are expected to be higher than today.
  • Frames US$55.00 as a fair value that aligns with more cautious analysts, who see the current share price as close to or above what those assumptions support.

data.title: Kosmos Energy (KOS) Deep Q4 EPS Loss Reinforces Bearish Profitability Narratives
data.description:
Kosmos Energy (KOS) has put a tough year on the tape, closing FY 2025 with Q4 revenue of US$294.9 million and a basic EPS loss of US$0.79 as net income excluding extra items came in at a loss of US$377.1 million. Over the trailing twelve months, the company has seen total revenue of about US$1.3 billion with a basic EPS loss of US$1.47 and a net loss excluding extra items of US$699.8 million, keeping the focus firmly on loss making operations rather than growth in the top line. With the stock trading at US$2.57 and margins under pressure, investors may frame this update around whether the current earnings weakness is acceptable in light of any potential rewards ahead.

See our full analysis for Kosmos Energy.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to see how this latest earnings run matches or challenges the main stories investors already have in mind about Kosmos.

See what the community is saying about Kosmos Energy

Advertisement

TTM loss of US$699.8 million despite US$1.3b in revenue

  • Over the last twelve months, Kosmos generated about US$1.3b in revenue but recorded a net loss excluding extra items of US$699.8 million, with basic EPS at a loss of US$1.47.
  • Bulls point to plans for higher output and margin improvement, yet these TTM figures show the business currently running at a sizeable loss, which contrasts with optimistic expectations of future free cash flow growth.
    • Optimistic views highlight potential margin expansion from projects like LNG and lower unit costs, while the latest year still reflects a loss of nearly US$700 million.
    • Forecasts that profitability is not expected within the next three years sit beside this loss profile, so any bullish case has to work against a recent history of negative EPS.

Bulls argue that the current loss making picture could set up a sharper inflection if cash flows ramp as planned, but the TTM numbers make it important to understand exactly what upside they are counting on. 🐂 Kosmos Energy Bull Case

Low 1x P/S and 7.9% discount to DCF fair value

  • Kosmos shares trade on a P/S of about 1x compared with 6.1x for peers and 1.8x for the US Oil & Gas industry, and the current price of US$2.57 is around 7.9% below a DCF fair value of roughly US$2.79.
  • Supporters of the bullish view see this valuation discount as a potential opportunity, while the recent record of losses means the low multiple can also be read as the market pricing in execution and profitability risks.
    • Revenue growth of about 3.8% per year is modest against the broader US market at 10.2% per year, so the P/S gap is not obviously explained by faster top line expansion.
    • The company has reduced losses only gradually over five years at around 0.5% per year, which helps explain why the market may not be willing to pay peer level multiples yet.

EPS trend from small loss to deeper Q4 hit

  • Quarterly basic EPS moved from a small loss of US$0.01 in Q4 2024 to a loss of US$0.23 in Q1 2025, US$0.18 in Q2, US$0.26 in Q3, then a deeper loss of US$0.79 in Q4 2025 as net income excluding extra items for that quarter came to a loss of US$377.1 million.
  • Bears argue this pattern backs a cautious stance, and the recent data lines up with their concerns about ongoing earnings pressure and balance sheet risk rather than a quick turnaround.
    • The trailing twelve month basic EPS loss of US$1.47 and TTM net loss of US$699.8 million sit alongside views that the company is not expected to reach profitability within three years.
    • Higher share price volatility in the last three months and significant insider selling over that period add to the cautious argument that recent financial pressure is feeding into how insiders and the market treat the stock.

Skeptics often focus on this widening EPS loss and recent volatility as reasons to treat the current P/S discount carefully rather than assuming it will close quickly. 🐻 Kosmos Energy Bear Case

data.title: Is It Too Late To Consider Garmin (GRMN) After A 24% Monthly Surge
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether Garmin's current share price matches its underlying worth, you are not alone. This article is here to help you size up what you are actually paying for.
  • The stock recently closed at US$250.69, with returns of 24.3% over 30 days, 23.8% year to date, 15.7% over 1 year, 171.0% over 3 years and 123.1% over 5 years, which sets an interesting backdrop for any valuation check.
  • Recent coverage has focused on Garmin's position in consumer durables and how investors are weighing its product portfolio, balance sheet strength and competitive standing against that share price. Together, these themes help frame whether the recent returns reflect changing expectations about the business or simply a shift in risk appetite around the stock.
  • Our valuation model currently gives Garmin a value score of 0 out of 6. Next we will break down what that means across different valuation approaches, and finish by looking at a more complete way to think about what the stock could be worth.

Garmin scores just 0/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Garmin Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A DCF model takes projected future cash flows, discounts them back to today using a required return, and adds them up to estimate what the entire company might be worth right now.

For Garmin, the model uses a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach based on cash flows in US$. The latest twelve month free cash flow is about $1.38b. Analysts provide detailed estimates for the next few years, then Simply Wall St extends those projections further out. By 2030, the forecast free cash flow used in the model is $2.34b, with a series of annual projections in between that are all discounted to today.

Adding those discounted amounts together gives an estimated intrinsic value of $232.82 per share. Compared with the recent share price of $250.69, the DCF outcome indicates that the stock is trading around 7.7% above that estimate, which falls into a reasonable band rather than an extreme gap.

Result: ABOUT RIGHT

Garmin is fairly valued according to our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF), but this can change at a moment's notice. Track the value in your watchlist or portfolio and be alerted on when to act.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Garmin.

Approach 2: Garmin Price vs Earnings (P/E)

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful shorthand because it links what you pay for each share to the earnings that support that price. Higher growth expectations or lower perceived risk usually justify a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher risk often line up with a lower, more cautious P/E.

Garmin currently trades on a P/E of about 29.0x. That sits above the Consumer Durables industry average of around 13.2x and also above its peer group average of about 21.2x. On the surface, that points to a premium price tag compared with both the wider industry and more direct peers.

Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for Garmin is 23.2x. This is a proprietary estimate of what a “normal” P/E might look like given the company’s earnings growth profile, industry, profit margins, market cap and risk characteristics. Because it folds these factors into a single number, the Fair Ratio can give a more tailored reference point than a simple comparison with broad industry or peer averages.

Setting the current P/E of 29.0x against the Fair Ratio of 23.2x suggests Garmin’s shares are pricing in more optimistic conditions than that model implies.

Result: OVERVALUED

Earlier we mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation, so let us introduce you to Narratives. These are simply your own story about Garmin that connects what you believe about its future revenue, earnings and margins to a financial forecast and Fair Value. You can then compare that to today’s price so you can judge whether the stock looks attractive. All of this is available within an easy tool on Simply Wall St’s Community page that updates as news and earnings arrive. Narratives can range from a bullish Garmin view with a Fair Value of US$310.0 per share to a more cautious view at US$185.0 per share, with the consensus Narrative in between at US$235.25, showing how different investors can look at the same company and reach very different but clearly quantified conclusions.

For Garmin, however, we’ll make it really easy for you with previews of two leading Garmin Narratives:

🐂 Garmin Bull Case

Fair Value in this bullish Narrative: US$310.00 per share

Implied discount to this Fair Value at the recent US$250.69 share price: about 19.1%

Revenue growth assumption used in this Narrative: 11.32% a year

  • Builds on analysts at the high end of the forecast range, who model higher revenue, slightly lower margins, and a higher future P/E multiple to support a Fair Value of US$310.0 and a bullish price target of US$285.0.
  • Flags real risks around smartphone substitution, heavy competition from large tech players, product concentration, pricing pressure, and regulation, and encourages you to test whether those bullish assumptions feel realistic.

🐻 Garmin Bear Case

Fair Value in this more cautious Narrative: US$235.25 per share

Implied premium to this Fair Value at the recent US$250.69 share price: about 6.6%

Revenue growth assumption used in this Narrative: 7.73% a year

  • Leans on the analyst consensus view that Garmin can still grow earnings through premium wearables, services, and aviation, but with more moderate revenue growth and margins than the bullish case.
  • Uses a Fair Value of US$235.25 and a consensus price target of about US$213.83, which together suggest the current P/E and share price assume a relatively full outcome for execution.
  • Highlights risks tied to trade policy, Marine and Outdoor softness, higher operating expenses, and foreign currency swings, which could make it harder to justify a premium valuation if they persist.

Mistral AI gives Accenture access to a focused suite of large language models that can be tailored to regional rules, including in Europe, which may matter for clients weighing data privacy and regulatory concerns. At the same time, bringing in Ookla’s Speedtest, Downdetector, Ekahau and RootMetrics data sets could help Accenture advise telecom operators, hyperscalers and large enterprises on how AI-powered tools sit on top of real world network performance rather than ideal lab conditions.

Plug Power FY 2025 Results: Revenue Growth With Deepening Losses

Plug Power (PLUG) closed FY 2025 with Q4 revenue of US$225.2 million and a basic EPS loss of US$0.63, alongside net income excluding extraordinary items of a US$846.0 million loss. This puts a sharp spotlight on the trade off between scale and profitability. Over recent periods the company has seen revenue move from US$191.5 million in Q4 2024 to US$225.2 million in Q4 2025, while basic EPS shifted from a loss of US$1.48 to a loss of US$0.63. This sets up a results season where investors are watching whether higher sales can eventually support cleaner margins.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to set these results against the widely shared narratives around Plug Power's growth ambitions, risk profile and path to better margins to see which stories hold up and which start to crack.

Losses Stay Heavy At Over US$1.6b On A 12‑Month View

  • Over the last twelve months, Plug Power booked about US$709.9 million in revenue and a net loss of roughly US$1.6b, which works out to a basic EPS loss of US$1.42 on a trailing basis.
  • Consensus narrative talks about policy support and operational improvements helping margins, yet the trailing net loss of about US$1.6b against under US$1.0b of revenue keeps profitability firmly in the red, which means:
    • The idea of margin progress is running against the reality that profit growth comparisons are still not very meaningful while the company is loss making at this scale.
    • With EPS negative over the last year, the story analysts outline around margin improvement needs sustained follow through before it shows up clearly in per share outcomes.

Short Cash Runway And Dilution Keep Pressure On

  • The risk summary flags less than one year of cash runway and substantial shareholder dilution over the past year, alongside a share price of US$2.23 that has been more volatile than the wider US market in recent months.
  • Bears focus on cash burn and funding needs, and the trailing figures support those worries, because:
    • Running a loss of about US$1.6b over the last twelve months with under a year of cash coverage lines up with concerns that further external financing could be needed.
    • Substantial dilution already reported over the past year fits the cautious view that future capital raising could keep weighing on existing holders on a per share basis.
If you are trying to weigh those cash burn and dilution risks against your own expectations, it is worth seeing how skeptics frame the downside case in more detail. 🐻 Plug Power Bear Case

Premium P/S Multiple Relies On Revenue Growth Story

  • Plug Power is trading on a P/S ratio of about 4.4x, compared with a peer average around 1.3x and a US Electrical industry average near 2.5x. Analysts expect revenue to grow roughly 19.2% per year, faster than the US market forecast of about 10.2% per year.
  • Bulls lean on that faster revenue growth to justify the richer sales multiple, but the current numbers give a mixed read, because:
    • The latest trailing revenue of roughly US$709.9 million versus a P/S of 4.4x points to a market valuation that is already assuming the higher growth path plays out.
